ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03311069 covers dosa as a general category; kal thosai (stone-griddle variant) is a subtype not separately enumerated.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP covers dosa broadly; kal thosai is a thicker stone-griddle preparation not separately coded.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept exists for kal thosai or dosa as a food product at this time.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of kal thosai is 2 pieces, each approximately 100 g, totalling 200 g, as typically served at South Indian restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Dosa (plain, fermented rice and lentil pancake) — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN reports plain dosa (fermented rice-lentil batter, griddle-cooked). Kal thosai uses the same batter but is cooked on a stone griddle (kal = stone in Tamil), yielding a slightly thicker, softer texture. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN plain dosa values; macros are consistent with the same batter composition. No curry or chutney is included per the input description.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of South Indian fermented foods: dosa and idli
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Peer-reviewed literature (Gopalpura et al.; Wolever et al. referencing fermented rice-lentil preparations)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for kal thosai found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 51 is estimated from published GI values for plain fermented dosa (range 51–66 across studies; Gopalpura et al. 2010, Diabetes Care; University of Sydney GI Database entries for plain dosa). The lower end of the range (51) is used as kal thosai uses the same fermented rice-lentil batter as plain dosa. GL per serving = (51 × 59.0 g available carbohydrate per 200 g serving) / 100 = 30.1.

hypertension.guidance: Kal thosai contains approximately 210 mg sodium per 100 g (420 mg per 2-piece serving); individuals with hypertension should monitor portion size and avoid high-sodium accompaniments such as pickles or salted chutneys to keep total sodium within the recommended 1500–2000 mg/day limit.

type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 51 (low) and a glycemic load of ~30 per 2-piece serving, kal thosai produces a moderate postprandial glucose response; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit to one piece per meal, pair with high-fibre vegetable accompaniments, and avoid sweetened chutneys to manage blood glucose effectively.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat and cholesterol-free profile
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Kal thosai is cholesterol-free and low in saturated fat (~0.7 g/100 g), making it a heart-friendly staple; however, the cooking oil used on the griddle should be a unsaturated-fat oil (e.g., sunflower or canola) and portion size should be controlled to limit total caloric intake in individuals with cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kal thosai (also spelled kal dosai or kal dosa) is a South Indian fermented rice-and-lentil pancake cooked on a traditional stone griddle — 'kal' meaning 'stone' in Tamil. It is made from a batter of parboiled rice and urad dal (black gram lentils) that has been fermented overnight, giving the pancake its characteristic slight sourness, soft and spongy interior, and lightly crisp exterior. Unlike the thin, crispy paper dosa, kal thosai is thicker and softer, with a more substantial bite. It is a staple of South Indian and Singapore Indian cuisine, commonly served for breakfast or as a light meal, typically accompanied by sambar (lentil vegetable stew) and coconut chutney, though in this record no curries are included. Nutritionally, kal thosai is a moderate-energy, low-fat, gluten-free, vegan food providing complex carbohydrates, modest plant protein from the lentil component, and B vitamins including thiamine, riboflavin, and folate. The fermentation process improves digestibility and bioavailability of nutrients. With a glycemic index of approximately 51 (low GI), it produces a moderate postprandial glucose response compared to plain white rice. It is suitable for vegetarian, vegan, and halal diets. Clinically, it is a reasonable carbohydrate source for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes when portion-controlled, and its low saturated fat content makes it appropriate for cardiovascular health. Sodium content (~210 mg/100 g) warrants attention for hypertensive individuals, particularly when paired with salty accompaniments.
common_user_queries: Is kal thosai the same as dosa?
How many calories in kal thosai?
Is kal thosai good for diabetics?
What is the GI of dosa?
How is kal thosai different from paper dosa?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN plain dosa data, which uses the same fermented rice-lentil batter as kal thosai. The stone-griddle cooking method does not materially alter macronutrient composition. GI is estimated from published analogous dosa GI values from the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature. No exact kal thosai entry exists in any approved primary source.
notes: Serving size set to 200 g (2 × 100 g pieces) based on the input record specifying 2 PCS and typical restaurant portion weights for kal thosai in Singapore. No curries or chutneys are included per the input description ('NO CURRIES PROVIDED'). Vitamin A is -1 as it is not separately reported for plain dosa in ICMR-NIN. Vitamin B6 is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN dosa entry. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for this food. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN dosa entry. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN dosa entry. Trans fat is 0.0 — confirmed zero as this is a plant-based fermented food with no hydrogenated fats. Cholesterol is 0.0 — confirmed zero as kal thosai contains no animal-derived ingredients. Vitamin C is 0.0 — confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN for plain dosa. Vitamin B12 is 0.0 — confirmed zero as this is a plant-based food with no animal-derived ingredients. GL per serving calculated as: GI (51) × available carbohydrate per serving (59.0 g = 29.5 g/100 g × 200 g) / 100 = 30.09, rounded to 30.1.
